svg.text: • Transitive closure (Warshall's algorithm): reachability in DAGs and DBs.
• Bottleneck / widest path: max-min over edge weights — same skeleton, swap operators.
• Regex → DFA conversion: Kleene's algorithm uses Floyd-Warshall over regex semiring.
• Network reliability: probability of connectivity over edge survival probabilities.
• Graph diameter / center / radius: compute all-pairs distances then aggregate.
• Counting paths between all pairs: matrix powers under (+, ×) semiring.
• Game pathfinding (small maps): APSP precomputation for sub-microsecond lookup.
• Sparse graphs with V > ~5000: Johnson's or repeated Dijkstra wins.
• When you only need a few queries: O(V³) preprocessing wastes work; do it on demand.
• Memory-constrained: O(V²) matrix can dominate. V = 50,000 → 20 GB at int64.
• Dynamic graphs: any edge change costs O(V²) to repair — full rerun is O(V³).
• Streaming / online updates: incremental APSP algorithms (Demetrescu-Italiano) are better.
• Very large dense graphs: blocked / parallel APSP (cache-aware tile decomposition).
V³ is the default; remember when something specific to your structure beats it.
